coredata.dc:title: Improved corrosion resistance on Mg-2Ca alloy with TiO2 nanoparticles embedded in a polycaprolactone (PCL) coating
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Alves, Marta M.
Santos, Catarina
Montemor, M.F.
coredata.dc:description: When seeking bone healing applications, calcium (Ca) is the preferred element for Magnesium (Mg) alloying. Unfortunately, the fast degradation rate of these alloys limits their applicability as bioresorbable implants. Biodegradable polymeric coatings based on polycaprolactone (PCL) were prepared to overcome this limitation. TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) were further embedded in the polymeric matrix to increase the protectiveness and biocompatibility of such coatings. The degradation rate, assessed by electrochemical impedance technique (EIS) in cell culture medium, proved the superior protectiveness of the PCL-TiO2 coating. The corrosion layer was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). In the bare and PCL coated samples, the corrosion layer was composed of brucite, carbonates, and magnesium oxychloride, whereas in the PCL-TiO2 coated sample, the additional precipitation of apatites occurred. This data advances important insights on Mg-2Ca alloys and discusses the role of bioactive coatings on the degradation of this alloy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Coating
Mg-Ca alloys
Nanoparticles
Polycaprolactone
TiO2

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Physicochemical characterization of PCL and PCL-TiO2 coatings

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2022 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Physicochemical characterization of bare and coated Mg-2Ca; top view scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of a) bare, b) PCL coated or c) PCL-TiO2 coated alloy where the upper inset is a magnified view of the coating with bright spheres corresponding to TiO2 NPs, and the lower inset is a magnified view of TiO2 NPs within the coating; cross-section view SEM images of d) PCL coated or e) PCL-TiO2 coated alloy were arrows refer to the coating thickness; EDS area analyses of f) bare, g) PCL coated or h) PCL-TiO2 coated alloy; 3D surface plot of i) bare, j) PCL coated or k) PCL-TiO2 coated alloy.

Morphological characterization of the bare and coated Mg-2Ca upon a 96 h immersion in cell culture medium; optical top view imaging of a) bare, b) PCL coated, or c) PCL-TiO2 coated alloy and optical cross-section view imaging of d) bare, e) PCL coated, or f) PCL-TiO2 coated alloy.

Electrochemical behavior of the bare and coated Mg-2Ca alloy when immersed in cell culture medium at 37 °C; a-c) Nyquist plots and d-f) Bode plots after 0 h (□), 24 h (■), 48 h (○), 72 h (●) and 96 h (∇) of immersion; g-i) phase angle plots collected upon immersion every 3 h up to 96 h.

Physicochemical characterization of the corrosion layer formed on the surface of Mg-2Ca 96 h after immersion; a) EDS spectrum, b) scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images where the right-hand images are magnified views of the areas marked on the left-hand image, and c) corresponding EDS maps.

Physicochemical characterization of the corrosion layer formed on the surface of coated Mg-2Ca 96 h after immersion; EDS spectra of a) PCL coated or d) PCL-TiO2 NPs coated alloy; scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images of b) PCL coated or e) PCL-TiO2 NPs coated alloy where the right-hand images are magnified views of the areas marked on the left-hand image, and corresponding c and f) EDS maps.
Weight composition of the as-cast Mg-2Ca alloy.

EDS analysis was performed on representative morphologies formed on the surface of the samples 96 h after immersion (Fig. 4 and 5); the EDS analyses were performed on the areas marked with i-iii) in Fig. 4b), on the areas marked with iv-vi) in Fig. 5b) and vii-ix in Fig. 5f), (–) not detected.

head.ce:title: Improved corrosion resistance on Mg-2Ca alloy with TiO2 nanoparticles embedded in a polycaprolactone (PCL) coating

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Instituto Superior Técnico, Av. Rovisco Pais

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: When seeking bone healing applications, calcium (Ca) is the preferred element for Magnesium (Mg) alloying. Unfortunately, the fast degradation rate of these alloys limits their applicability as bioresorbable implants. Biodegradable polymeric coatings based on polycaprolactone (PCL) were prepared to overcome this limitation. TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) were further embedded in the polymeric matrix to increase the protectiveness and biocompatibility of such coatings. The degradation rate, assessed by electrochemical impedance technique (EIS) in cell culture medium, proved the superior protectiveness of the PCL-TiO2 coating. The corrosion layer was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). In the bare and PCL coated samples, the corrosion layer was composed of brucite, carbonates, and magnesium oxychloride, whereas in the PCL-TiO2 coated sample, the additional precipitation of apatites occurred. This data advances important insights on Mg-2Ca alloys and discusses the role of bioactive coatings on the degradation of this alloy.

ce:section.ce:para: Nanoscale Titanium dioxide (TiO2) is a well-known additive widely used as a bioactive material with the potential for tissue regeneration, osseointegration, and minimized bacterial accumulation [1]. Chemical inertness properties, non-toxicity, and low cost are also important drivers for using this material [2]. Moreover, TiO2 has been approved by the American Food and Drug Administration (FDA) for use in human food contact materials [3]. Due to this panoply of properties, extensive research has been carried out on the applicability of TiO2 in biomaterials, namely for osteoconductive and antimicrobial applications [4–8].

The proven bioactivity of TiO2 and its corrosion protectiveness led to the design of TiO2 coatings to enhance the bioactivity of Mg implants and prevent the fast degradation of this biodegradable material [4,9,10]. However, controversy and intense debate still exist on this matter. For example, Morquecho-Marín et al. [11] reported that a TiO2 coating increased the corrosion rate of an Mg alloy. Like pure TiO2 coatings, ceramic coatings with TiO2 nanoparticles (NPs) have been attempted on Mg alloys [12,13]. Still, their corrosion protection is far from satisfying due to the risk of flaws and localized failures [14]. TiO2 plays a unique role in polymeric matrices in composite polymeric coatings as it contributes to the synthesis of high-performance and malleable networks [2]. From the various biodegradable polymeric coatings, polycaprolactone (PCL), a biocompatible polymer that U.S. Food and Drug Administration approved as an implantable material, has been used to control the degradation rate of Mg implants [15]. However, the polymer hydrolysis leads to local pH acidification that further accelerates the corrosion of the underneath Mg alloy [15]. Moreover, PCL has an intrinsic hydrophobic nature, and thus the poor surface wetting limits the interaction with biological fluids by avoiding cells’ adhesion and proliferation [15,16]. The combination of PCL with TiO2 NPs may overcome these drawbacks and bring advantages in terms of coating biocompatibility and alloy corrosion resistance. The design of PCL-TiO2 coatings with other additives such as hydroxyapatite or MgO resulted in an efficient corrosion-resistant barrier of Mg alloys [16,17].

Several Mg alloys have been put-forward as implantable materials for bone healing applications (e.g. [18]). The standard alloys, such as AZ31 and AZ91, have Al as an alloying element, which has been responsible for detrimental health effects upon alloys’ degradation [19]. In the quest for more biocompatible Mg alloys, Mg-Ca alloys, thanks to their inherent chemical composition, are attracting attention due to their beneficial healing effects and physicochemical properties [20–23]. Calcium increases the material's biocompatibility and can increase the mechanical resistance of solid solutions and precipitates. To a large extent, it can also act as a grain refining agent and contribute to grain boundary strengthening. In Mg-Ca binary alloys, intermetallic phases with the composition of Mg2Ca are formed, having the creep resistance increase as a result [20–24]. This second phase is placed at the grain boundaries, generating grain boundary pinning and refining [24]. The microstructure of Mg-Ca alloys changes according to Ca solubility in Mg. Typically, below Ca maximum solubility in Mg, i.e., 1.34% (w/w), binary Mg-Ca alloys contain an α-Mg matrix and Mg2Ca as the second phase; for higher Ca contents, the combination of Mg2Ca with α-Mg gives rise to the formation of the β-phase [25,26]. Most studies suggest that higher corrosion resistance is reached when the Ca content is below its maximum solubility in Mg [25,26]. However, increased Ca contents are desirable to favor bone-healing. In this quest, Rad et al. [27] have studied Mg–xCa alloys (x = 0.5, 1.25, 2.5, 5 and 10 wt%) and reported that increased corrosion rates occurred with growing Ca contents. This is due to the preferential formation of Mg2Ca intermetallic compounds in grain boundaries [27]. To overcome this problem, thermomechanical processing, alone or in combination with heat treatments has shown remarkable improvement in biocorrosion as well as in mechanical properties of Mg alloys [28]. Jeong et al. [29] reported that increased Ca contents decreased the corrosion resistance of as-cast Mg–xCa (x = 2–3 wt%) alloys, but after extrusion, an anodic reaction deceleration resulted in a significant improvement of the corrosion resistance compared with their as-cast counterparts. Similarly, Li et al. [30] proved that hot rolling or as-extrusion improved the corrosion resistance of Mg–xCa (x = 1–3 wt.%) alloys in simulated physiological conditions. By combining mechanical and thermal treatments, Seong et al. [31] improved the performance of Mg-Ca alloys for Ca levels as high as 2–3% (w/w). Such Mg-Ca alloys, by ensuring a constant Ca supply during the implant degradation, can support bone recovery along the entire healing process. This is an approach that goes beyond the state-of-the-art, where Ca has been mainly provided in a short transient manner in biodegradable coatings on other Mg alloys [16,32].

Despite the good mechanical properties and biocompatibility of Mg-Ca alloys, the corrosion rate is still too high to match the bone healing process [33]. Decreased corrosion rates were achieved by micro-arc oxidation (MAO) of Mg−xCa (x = 0.5, 0.8, 1.3 and 4.5 wt.%) alloys [34,35], by plasma electrolytic oxidation (PEO) of Mg−0.8Ca [36] and by Mg−Al layered double hydroxides (LDH) treatment on Mg−xCa (x = 0.5, 0.8 and 2.0 wt.%) alloys [37]. Calcium phosphate and TiO2 coatings were also reported to improve the corrosion resistance of the Mg-1.0Ca alloy [38–40]. A double-layered composite coating of nano-hydroxyapatite and PCL was as well reported to improve the corrosion resistance of Mg-1.2Ca alloy [41]. To the authors’ best knowledge, no inorganic functionalized polymeric coating has been reported on Mg-Ca alloys. To delay the corrosion rate of promising Mg-2Ca alloys for bone-healing applications [31], a new functionalized polymeric coating of PCL-TiO2 was designed by dip-coating techniques and compared with a PCL coating without additives. The corrosion resistance of the coated alloys was studied by electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS), the morphology of the degradation products was characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and their chemical composition was evaluated by energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS).

An Mg-Ca alloy with 2% (w/w) of Ca was fabricated at CanmetMATERIALS laboratories. After casting in a steel mold, the alloy was homogenized in an argon atmosphere following the temperature profile presented in Sup. Fig. 1 a). The weight composition of the resulting alloy is shown in Table 1 .

For the PCL-TiO2 coating formulation, a commercial 10% (w/v) water-based solution of TiO2 NPs (Sigma-Aldrich; Sup. Fig. 1b) was added to a PCL concentration of 0.05% (v/v). All the PCL-based coatings were applied on the Mg-Ca alloy with a dip coater (RDC-15, Bungard); samples were immersed in PCL-based solutions for 10 s and withdrawn with a speed of 0.8 mm s−1.

In the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) experiments, to mimic bone implants, a low volume-to-surface area ratio was used, i. e., a surface area of 3.14 cm2 was exposed to 10 mL of a cell culture medium, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM, Sigma-Aldrich) with a pH of 7.4 at 37 °C. The10 mV rms perturbation and a frequency swept from 105 Hz down to 10−2 Hz were applied with a reference 600+ (Gamry Instruments). The EIS spectra were collected periodically at open circuit potential (OCP) every 3 h up to 96 h of immersion. In the three-electrode electrochemical cell, assembly used, Mg-2Ca coupons (bare or coated) were used as working electrodes (WE), saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as the reference electrode, and a Pt coil as the counter electrode (CE). Samples were measured in duplicate, and data analysis was made with Zview software (Scribner Associates, Inc.).

The physicochemical characterization of the bare or coated samples, before and after immersion, was performed with an optical magnifier (Leica DMS 300) and by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) using a JEOL-JSM7001F, Hitachi S2400, or Phenom ProX G6 apparatus. For the EDS analyses, either an Oxford INCA 250 or Bruker Esprit light elements detectors were used at accelerating voltages of 25 kV. The 3D plots and surface roughness were calculated based on the collected SEM images and processed with the ImageJ software (ImageJ v1.51j8, USA) plugins, interactive 3D surface plots, and roughness calculation, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physicochemical characterization of PCL and PCL-TiO2 coatings
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 1 provides a comprehensive set of results on the physicochemical characterization of the bare Mg-2Ca and coated alloy.

As depicted in Fig. 1a), the darker surface of the bare alloy contrasts with the whiter surface of the polymeric coatings (Fig. 1b, c). The surface of these coatings is very homogenous, without relevant cracks or other defects, as can be seen in Fig. 1b and c). Our data clearly contrasts with the reported PCL coating designed on an Mg-1Ca alloy, where interconnected pore networks were formed upon solvent evaporation [41]. This is due to the molecular weight and wt% of PCL as well as the solvent used, showing that the combination used herein proved better than the conjunction reported by Bakhsheshi-Rad et al. [41]. When TiO2 NPs are added to the PCL coating, polymeric protuberances are formed on the surface (Fig. 1c). This is due to the coagulation process caused by the water-based solution [42] of the NPs, which results in the formation of structures with diameters of around 4 µm (Sup. Fig. 3 ). The existence of TiO2 NPs (Sup. Fig. 1b) on the coating was addressed by the presence of bright and well-dispersed entities (upper inset in Fig. 1c) that present high Ti contents as confirmed by localized EDS analysis (data not shown). Higher magnification of such bright entities revealed that the TiO2 NPs were dispersed in the coating in the form of NPs aggregates, as seen in the lower inset of Fig. 1c. To analyze the thickness of the coatings, cross-section images were acquired for both coated samples (Fig. 1d, e). Both coatings presented an average thickness of around 1 µm, suggesting that the addition of the water-based NPs solution did not influence the PCL-TiO2 coating thickness (Fig. 1e) compared with PCL alone (Fig. 1d).

The chemical composition of the samples was analyzed by EDS. The EDS spectra of the bare and coated samples revealed the presence of Mg and Ca from the alloy (Fig. 1f-h). In the bare alloy, the additional presence of O (Fig. 1f) relates to the formation of superficial oxide/hydroxide layers [43]. The additional presence of C in the PCL coated samples, together with an increased O content, is a consequence of the polymeric coating (Fig. 1g, h). The addition of TiO2 NPs into the PCL coating was well proven by the presence of Ti in the EDS analysis (Fig. 1h).

Roughness is essential to ensure adequate cell adhesion [44,45]; thus, SEM images were processed into 3D surface plots (Fig. 1i-k). The bare surface presented an average roughness (Ra) of 135.8 ± 0.6 µm; for the PCL coated samples, the Ra was 66.7 ± 0.8 µm, whereas, for the PCL-TiO2 coating, the Ra was about 74 ± 1 µm. As shown in Fig. 1i-k, the roughness of the bare (Fig. 1i) and PCL coated (Fig. 1j) samples were derived by the still visible underneath surface finishing scratches formed upon samples’ polishing. In contrast, in the PCL-TiO2 coating, the presence of the polymeric protuberances and TiO2 NPs hindered the scratches (Fig. 1k).

In the case of orthopedic implants, rapid degradation of the alloy surface will impact the physiological behavior of the surrounding bone tissues [46]. To assess the degradation behavior of the bare and coated alloy, the complexity of the physiological media was mimicked by using a cell culture medium instead of the traditional salt solution reported on Mg-based materials coated with polymeric matrixes and TiO2 [14,16,17].

Fig. 2a, d) shows that a thick layer of corrosion products was formed on the bare alloy. Unlike the bare alloy, in both coated samples, the substrate (darker regions in Fig. 2b, c) was still perceptible underneath the corrosion products developed (whiter-pink regions in Fig. 2b, c). Development of pits, typically formed during Mg alloys’ degradation [47] is evident on the bare alloy (Fig. 2d), proving that an active corrosion process occurred upon immersion. Smaller pits are perceptible on the alloy coated with PCL (Fig. 2e) and less perceptible pitting formation is depicted on the PCL-TiO2 coated alloy (Fig. 2f). All the corroded surfaces present a pink tone on the corrosion layers formed (Fig. 2). This results from the absorption of the pH indicator on DMEM that, due to the typical alkalinization that occurred during Mg degradation [43,47], turns into a solid pink color.

Electrochemical experiments were also carried out to have a deeper insight into the degradation behavior of the bare and coated alloys (Sup. Fig. 4 , Fig. 3). The bare and coated alloys' open circuit potential (OCP) increased in the first hours upon immersion, achieving a nearly stable plateau around −1.6 mV (Sup. Fig. 4). When comparing the values of the OCP plateaux of the coated samples with those of the bare alloy, the coated ones presented more positive OCP values when compared to the uncoated alloy (Sup. Fig. 4). To further explore these alterations, the degradation behavior of the samples was investigated by EIS (Fig. 3). The Nyquist plots obtained on the bare alloy (Fig. 3a) evidence a larger capacitive loop upon immersion. The larger diameter of this capacitive loop accounts for higher polarization resistances and therefore decreased surface activity. This is most likely due to a protective native oxide layer formed at the physiological pH of 7.4 [31], supported by EDS measurements (Fig. 1d), that provided surface protection to the alloy at the early stages of immersion. Upon degradation, intense corrosion activity resulted in a sharp drop of the impedance 24 h after immersion; this trend was kept up to 96 h of immersion (Fig. 3a). Similar behavior was observed for the PCL-coated sample (Fig. 3b). Despite the lower impedance values at early stages in the presence of the PCL coating, the impedance values observed at later stages were higher than those of the bare alloy, evidencing that the PCL coating slightly delayed the surface activity. The PCL-TiO2 coated alloy (Fig. 3c) revealed impedance values identical to those of the PCL-coated sample at the early stages of immersion. Still, there were some fluctuations over time, and the sharp drop was only observed 72 h after immersion. This trend suggests that the PCL-TiO2 coating introduces a more protective effect than the PCL alone, a result that agrees with the less attacked surface observed in Fig. 2.

The Bode plots (Fig. 3 d-f) clearly show that the most significant impedance drop occurred in the bare alloy for which |Z| decreased from 104 Ohm.cm2 to values of approximately 103 Ohm cm2 24 h after immersion (Fig. 3d); for the PCL coated samples identical values were observed, i.e., 103 Ohm cm2 but these were reached at a later stage of immersion, i.e., after approximately 48 h (Fig. 3e). For the PCL-TiO2, despite some low-frequency fluctuations, higher |Z| values were observed at the end of the test, i.e., after 96 h of immersion (Fig. 3f).

To further detail the ongoing degradation process of the samples, the phase angle values were collected every 3 h (Fig. 3g-i). In all three samples, two-time constants were clearly visible at earlier immersion stages, one at higher frequencies representing the protective surface layer on the bare substrate (Fig. 3g) or the polymeric matrix on the coated samples (Fig. 3h, i). The time constant can be assigned to the double layer and faradic resistance at a lower frequency range. At very low frequencies, the spectra are distorted by inductive loops, which are typical of Mg corrosion. That might be related to the relaxation of adsorbed intermediates involved in the degradation process [47,48].

For the PCL-TiO2 coated sample, the two-time constants, related to the coating and double-layer and faradic resistance, although with smaller phase angle values, are kept along the immersion time (Fig. 3i). For both the PCL and bare alloy, the low-frequency time constant shifts towards the middle frequency range (Fig. 3g, h). This effect, which occurs earlier for bare Mg-2Ca, and later for the PCL coated alloy, can be assigned to forming an intermediate layer composed of the growing corrosion products (Fig. 2) that decreases the active surface area the nature of the native surface. At higher frequencies, the increased impedance and the more negative phase angle values corroborate a layer of corrosion products that confers little protection.

The degradation products formed on the bare and coated samples upon immersion in DMEM were analyzed by SEM imaging, for their morphological characterization, and EDS to determine their elemental composition (Fig. 4 and 5).

Mg-2Ca alloys, due to their chemical composition and nontoxic degradation products, are up-and-coming absorbable materials for bone healing applications. Still, their fast degradation under cell culture medium represents a drawback in its applicability. This work demonstrates that the rapid degradation rate of this alloy can be delayed by applying a PCL coating with embedded TiO2 NPs. The presence of TiO2 NPs altered the surface roughness compared to PCL alone. The PCL-TiO2 coating, besides a reduction in the corrosion rate of the alloy, forms a biocompatible corrosion layer composed of brucite, carbonates, and apatites in cell culture medium (DMEM) 96 h after immersion. Therefore, PCL-TiO2 coatings show the potential to delay the degradation of Mg-2Ca while potentially increasing its biocompatibility. Despite these advances, the biodegradability of the alloy and the polymeric matrix contrasts with the prevalence of TiO2 NPs. From a future perspective, studies on the advantages of using TiO2 over its detrimental effects in wound-healed sites are required to demonstrate the feasibility of this approach.

Marta M. Alves: Methodology, Validation, Investigation, Conceptualization, Writing – original draft. Catarina Santos: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. M.F. Montemor: Conceptualization, Validation, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have influenced the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge UIDB/00100/2020 (Funded by FCT). They thank both Dr. Haiou Jin, from CANMET Materials Technology Laboratory, Dr. Shahrzad Esmaeili, also from CANMET Materials Technology Laboratory, and the University of Waterloo, from Canada, for their precious help with the alloy design.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.apsadv.2022.100257.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Antibacterial and bioactive surface modifications of titanium implants by PCL/TiO2 nanocomposite coatings
Nanomaterials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.S.K. Kiran, T.S.S. Kumar, R. Sanghavi, M. Doble, S. Ramakrishna, Antibacterial and bioactive surface modifications of titanium implants by PCL/TiO2 nanocomposite coatings, Nanomaterials, 8 (2018) 860.

Suchea (Eds.), Functional Nanostructured Interfaces For Environmental and Biomedical Applications, Elsevier, 2019, pp. 323–339.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unexpected cytotoxicity of TiO2-coated magnesium alloys
Mater. Lett.
